Conservation · Fieldwork · Beyond Work

Documenting Planted & Wild Trees

Baseline for a Conservation Project

Location Yarroway Farm, HD Kote Taluk, Mysuru District
01 — The Farm

Yarroway Farm

The farm is a biodynamic landscape that has been regenerating its soils since 2017, focusing on restoring soil health while supporting local biodiversity and wildlife.

Its practices follow natural rhythms — of seasons, planetary cycles, and the ebb and flow of the Nugu Reservoir.

With a long-term vision, the farm aims to leave behind deeper, richer soil and land capable of supporting a more abundant and diverse ecosystem for future generations.

Conservation Project

As a first step to start a conservation project, the farm aimed to understand how planted trees and the wild trees restore soil health organically and how they help ecosystems flourish.

This is where I became part of the project, contributing by documenting the tree diversity.

Coming from a completely different background, documenting trees for the first time and identifying species using bark, leaves, and illustrated tree guides was a rewarding and engaging experience that opened me to a new way of observing nature.

02 — Fieldwork Insights & Impact

Understanding the Landscape

The documentation focused on how planted trees facilitate natural forest regeneration over time, supporting the growth of native and wild species. Between 2015 and 2025, the landscape transitioned from 96 existing wild trees to an ecosystem of 2,000+ planted and 2,000+ wild trees, establishing a baseline for the conservation initiative.

22×
Increase in wild tree count since 2015 (125%)
1.2:1
Near-balanced planted-to-wild ratio, strengthening topsoil
4,000+
Total trees across planted and wild species by 2025

Increase in Naturally Occurring Trees Over Time

Wild Species
Planted Species

Key Observations

  • Native and wild tree species consistently emerged around planted trees — an overall 22x increase (125%) in wild tree count since 2015, signalling active natural regeneration of the landscape.
  • The coexistence of planted and wild tree species resulted in a near-balanced planted-to-wild ratio (1.2:1), which helped rebuild and strengthen topsoil and overall ecosystem health.
  • The patterns observed reveal a promising strategy for guiding future conservation efforts.

Observed over 100s of naturally growing Neem trees and learned how their slow leaf decomposition enriches the soil and supports natural pest control with minimal inputs.

03 — From Digital to Living Systems

From Designing Digital Systems to Helping Restore Living Systems

Design isn't limited to screens or products — it shows up in the way we observe, organise, and make sense of what's around us. While documenting and identifying tree species on the farm, I approached the work much like a design problem — recording everything I observed, structuring the information, and comparing it across years to understand change.

The context was different, but the way of thinking felt familiar.

What I designed in tech What I applied in conservation
Designing systems
Interpreting the farm as a living system with dependencies and feedback loops
Working with data
Structuring raw field observations into usable insights on tree diversity
Designing for long-term outcomes
Building datasets that support future conservation decisions

Natural Regeneration Around Planted Trees for one of the Zones (2015, 2020 & 2025)

04 — Learning from the Land

Learning from the Land

I initially came to the farm to learn more about organic and biodynamic farming, and to understand how land can be cared for in a way where nourishment flows back to the land instead of being extracted from it. What began as curiosity quickly grew into a deeper engagement with the farm's conservation goals.

As I became involved in the conservation project, I found deep meaning in contributing to something larger than myself and began learning to read the land more closely. Among many insights, one that stood out was how plants often labelled as "unwanted" acted as indicators of soil condition and recovery, revealing how the land responds when given time and care.

This wasn't new knowledge, but knowledge that has often been misunderstood or overlooked.

This work affirmed my intention to support systems that allow both people and nature to thrive — thoughtfully, patiently, and with the next generation in mind.

05 — Readings & Influences

Readings & Influences

These are a few readings and movements that helped me see the land differently.

  • Braiding Sweetgrass — Robin Wall Kimmerer
  • Entangled Life — Merlin Sheldrake
  • Jadav Payeng (The Forest Man of India) — His decades-long effort of planting and protecting an entire forest by hand showcases the power of patience, care, and long-term commitment to land restoration.
  • Save Soil Movement — Highlighted that healthy soil is the base for life, farming, and the future.

Most importantly, it was working on the farm itself that truly shifted my perspective. Seeing the land respond, regenerate, and support life made the impact tangible.

After witnessing this firsthand, I don't wish to look away anymore — I want to stay engaged and contribute wherever I can.

Design isn't limited to screens or products — it shows up in the way we observe, organise, and make sense of what's around us.
